steps.title: Track the wave contrast
steps.content: The passage contrasts ordinary traveling waves with spike waves. Traveling waves break once steepness passes a threshold, which limits their height. McAllister et al. studied spike waves, which form when traveling waves moving in opposing directions intersect and can erupt vertically.

steps.title: Focus on the study finding
steps.content: The key finding is that spike waves can exceed the traveling-wave height constraint because their breaking is shaped by more than steepness. Jet stability and cavity shape also matter, so the main idea must describe spike waves as behaving differently from what traveling-wave properties alone would predict.

steps.title: Reject distorted summaries
steps.content: Choice A wrongly applies jet stability and cavity shape to traveling waves. Choice B wrongly says spike waves exceed limits simply by reaching a steepness threshold. Choice C treats spike-wave formation as the researchers' main suggestion and adds a general height claim the text does not make. Choice D keeps the focus on intersecting traveling waves producing a wave that may be higher than expected. The answer is D.
